name: The Cooley Peninsula & Carlingford
description: A Walk Through Myth and Mountain. We begin in Carlingford, exploring King John's Castle and medieval laneways before heading to the mountains. The Cooley Way is a circular route; most walkers start north toward Omeath, a small lakeside village where the ferry to Warrenpoint departs daily. The trail climbs through woodland and moorland onto the Cooley ridge. This is the mythological heartland of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, the Cattle Raid of Cooley, one of the greatest epics in early Irish literature. Every valley and stream is named in this 2,000-year-old story; walking these paths means treading the landscape of myth. The ridge walk is one of Ireland's finest, with views constantly shifting as you move west. Lough Carlingford deepens below on your left; moorland rolls toward the Midlands on your right. Waymarking is excellent throughout. Slieve Foy at 589 metres is the walk's emotional centre. From here, the Mournes rise across the lough like a dark wall. The Midlands stretch to the horizon. On clear days, you can see the Dublin Mountains to the south.
short_description: Ireland's mythological heartland — the Cooley Mountains rising above a glacial fjord, the Mournes visible across Carlingford Lough, and one of Ireland's finest medieval harbour towns anchoring it all.
overview: The Cooley Peninsula sits at the northeastern edge of Leinster, shaped by glaciers and steeped in myth. Just 100 kilometres north of Dublin, it's one of Ireland's most underrated walking destinations. The Cooley Mountains reach 589 metres, and from Slieve Foy's summit you can see three countries: Ulster, the Midlands and the Mournes across Carlingford Lough. Carlingford Lough is a glacial fjord, one of Ireland's most beautiful bodies of water, ringed by mountains on three sides. Carlingford, the medieval town and gateway, is one of Europe's finest surviving medieval towns with King John's Castle, the Tholsel gate and partly visible town walls. The oysters are world-class and restaurants genuinely excellent.
landscape_description: The Cooley Mountains reach 589 metres; their position and exposure give them disproportionate drama. They rise above Carlingford Lough, a glacial fjord, which is 11 kilometres long, up to 4 kilometres wide. The underlying geology is sandstone, creating rounded grass-covered slopes. In late summer, heather blooms purple across moorland. Spring brings golden gorse. Ravensdale Forest to the south is mixed woodland of oak, ash and hazel, ancient and rich in birdlife. On windless mornings in late spring and early summer, the lough is mirror-still with complete mountain reflections. Red grouse inhabit higher moorland. Peregrine falcons nest above Slieve Foy. Harbour porpoises, seals and fish populate the lough itself.
cultural_highlights: The Cooley Peninsula's identity stems from early medieval Christianity, Norman-English medieval urbanisation and Irish epic literature. The Táin Bó Cúailnge, the Cattle Raid of Cooley, composed in the 7th and 8th centuries, tells of Queen Medb's war against Ulster to obtain the stud bull from Cooley. Walking the Cooley Way with knowledge of the Táin adds extraordinary depth; every stream, ridge and valley carries narrative weight. Carlingford is a physical text of medieval history. King John's Castle, built around 1190, controlled the lough. The Tholsel gate is one of Europe's finest surviving medieval town gates. Medieval street plans remain fundamentally unchanged from the 1400s. Carlingford oysters — farmed in clean lough water, among Ireland's finest; raw or gratinéed. Fresh seafood — mussels, crab and lobster caught by local fishermen, featured on every restaurant menu. Dundalk Bay prawns — regional speciality served simply grilled or in seafood pasta. Carlingford Brewing Company — craft ales and stouts brewed locally
difficulty_overview: The Cooley Way is moderate for those with reasonable fitness. Our 2–3 day itinerary covers 12–20 kilometres per day with daily ascents typically 300–500 metres. The route is well-waymarked and navigation straightforward even in poor visibility. The most demanding sections are open ridge walks, particularly to Slieve Foy and exposed moorland where wind is strong and weather changes quickly. The descent from Slieve Foy is steep but brief. Final stages approaching Omeath are gentler. The Carlingford Lough Greenway is entirely flat and easy, suitable for all fitness levels including families with children.
accommodation_style: Carlingford is the accommodation hub and perfectly suited to walkers. The town has small boutique hotels, quality guesthouses and family-run B&Bs, many in converted medieval buildings overlooking the lough. Quality is impressively consistent. Omeath, the second overnight stop, has smaller options with charming village setting. We pre-book quality accommodation with breakfast included, selecting properties that understand walker needs. Luggage transfers each morning; you carry only a daypack.
practical_info: Dublin Airport (DUB) is the primary international arrival point with direct services from North America, the Middle East and Europe. Carlingford is 100 kilometres north of Dublin, approximately 1.5 to 2 hours via the M1 motorway. Bus Éireann operates coach services from Dublin's Busáras station to Dundalk (1.5 hours), with connections to Carlingford. Irish Rail services Dublin Heuston to Dundalk (20 kilometres south); from Dundalk, Bus Éireann connects to Carlingford or local taxis can be arranged. Parking is available in town and at accommodation properties.
who_is_it_for: The Cooley Peninsula is perfect for shorter walking holidays—a long weekend or 4–5 days—without sacrificing scenery or genuine mountain walking. The compact size gives satisfaction without committing to a full week. It suits walkers interested in Irish mythology and early medieval history. Once you know the Táin Bó Cúailnge, every place name gains significance. Walking becomes a form of reading landscape as text. It's excellent for combining two destinations. The ferry from Omeath to Warrenpoint links to the Mourne Mountains, making 3 days on the Cooley Way and 3–4 days on the Mournes possible in a week-long itinerary for experienced walkers.
best_time_to_visit: April through October offers best walking conditions. April and May bring clear skies, bright light and quiet trails; gorse flowers and easy accommodation booking. June and early July offer longest daylight hours. August brings the Carlingford Oyster Festival, a culinary highlight. September is finest: bracken and heather turn gold and rust, light is clear and low, trails are quieter than summer, and accommodation books easily. October brings autumn colour to woodlands and sharp, clear days for photography. July and August are busiest; book accommodation 2–3 months in advance. Winter requires proper cold-weather gear and mountain experience.

name: Dublin & Wicklow
description: Dublin: City Walking and Literary Heritage. Dublin became a UNESCO City of Literature in 2010. Start at Trinity College, where Beckett, Wilde, Yeats and Swift studied; the library holds the Book of Kells. Walk south to Merrion Square, where Wilde lived and plaques mark the homes of writers and thinkers. The National Library on Kildare Street is essential for anyone interested in the city's literary history. A proper literary walking tour traces locations mentioned in Joyce's Ulysses — from Sweny's Pharmacy, now a Joyce museum, to places where Leopold Bloom walked on 16 June 1904, commemorated annually as Bloomsday. The General Post Office marks the heart of the 1916 Rising. Kilmainham Gaol, now a museum, is where the rebellion's leaders were executed, a moment that changed Irish history and resonates through the city to this day. Dublin's other cultural currency is its pub culture. These are neighbourhood institutions where writers, musicians, students and locals gather. Walk five minutes from the crowded Temple Bar and you'll find the real Dublin: quiet bars with excellent Guinness, live traditional music several nights a week, and conversations meandering from politics to literature to sport.
short_description: Georgian city streets, coastal paths and wild mountain moorland — all within 30 minutes of each other. Dublin's literary legacy meets the Wicklow Mountains in Europe's most accessible urban-to-wilderness combination.
overview: Few places offer both a great European capital and wilderness within 30 minutes of each other. Dublin's Georgian squares and literary pubs sit minutes from the Wicklow Mountains; those granite peaks rise dramatically south of the city, carved by glaciers into valleys and lakes that feel genuinely remote despite their proximity to two million people. This destination suits walkers who want both cultures at once. You can trace Joyce's Ulysses through Dublin's south side, spend an evening in a traditional pub hearing live music, then wake to a coastal walk above the Irish Sea from Dún Laoghaire to Bray before heading south to day-hike the Wicklow Mountains or join the full Wicklow Way trail. The combination is without equal in Ireland. The city's greatest writers — Joyce, Beckett, Wilde, Yeats — all walked these hills, leaving their fingerprints on every street corner and literary landmark.
landscape_description: South of the city, the granite Wicklow Mountains rise abruptly: glacial valleys, ribbon lakes and jagged ridges shaped over 500 million years. The coastal stretch from Dún Laoghaire to Bray alternates sandy beaches with rocky headlands, and Killiney Bay curves in a perfect crescent. Lower mountain slopes are oak and beech woodland; mid-levels open to heather bog; upper ridges above 600 metres offer views extending to Wales. Sika deer cross the moorland at dawn, peregrine falcons hunt the cliff faces, and otters inhabit the glacial lakes.
cultural_highlights: Dublin is a UNESCO City of Literature: Joyce, Beckett, Wilde and Yeats all walked these streets, and you can trace their footsteps on a literary walking tour. Kilmainham Gaol tells the 1916 Rising story with real power. The pub culture is central to Dublin life, with live traditional music most nights. For food, try Dublin Bay prawns, fresh oysters from the Irish Sea, and the weekend markets at Temple Bar and Dún Laoghaire. A DART trip to Howth for seafood at the harbour is one of our favourite recommendations.
difficulty_overview: City and coastal walks are easy to moderate, requiring only basic fitness and good shoes. Mountain day walks like the Spinc ridge and Three Rock are moderate to challenging, with ascents of 200 to 500 metres; proper waterproofs and ankle support are essential. The full Wicklow Way (8 days) is moderate overall.
accommodation_style: We book quality guesthouses and B&Bs across three zones: Dublin city centre for cultural access; coastal towns like Dún Laoghaire and Bray, served by the DART with no car needed; and mountain villages like Laragh and Roundwood near Glendalough, where family-run guesthouses understand walkers' needs. Breakfast is always included, and we arrange luggage transfers for multi-day itineraries.
practical_info: Dublin Airport (DUB) is one of Europe's most connected airports with direct services from North America, the Middle East and across Europe. The Airlink express bus connects the airport to O'Connell Street in approximately 25 to 30 minutes. From Dublin to Wicklow: Use the DART coastal railway to access walks at Dún Laoghaire, Dalkey, Killiney and Greystones; no car needed. Buy a Leap Card on arrival at the airport (covers all Dublin buses and the DART). Wicklow Mountains are 30 to 45 minutes by bus from Dublin city centre; ideal for day walks without overnight stays.
who_is_it_for: First-time visitors to Ireland who want city culture and wild landscapes without a full week of trekking. City-based walkers with 3 to 5 days who want variety: urban, coastal and mountain walking combined. Couples and groups with mixed abilities, since easy coastal routes sit alongside challenging mountain trails. Literary enthusiasts tracing Joyce, Beckett and Wilde through the streets that shaped their work. We also recommend this as a perfect bookend to the Wicklow Way: a celebratory few days in Dublin after a week in the mountains.
best_time_to_visit: The walking season runs April to October. May and June are our top picks: long evenings, wildflowers on the moorland, and lighter trail traffic. September offers golden light, quieter trails and easier booking. July and August are warmest but Glendalough gets crowded; book accommodation 2 to 3 months ahead. Winter walking is possible with proper gear, and Dublin's literary scene is excellent year-round.

name: The Mourne Mountains & County Down
description: The Range and Its Character. The Mourne Mountains cover approximately 570 square kilometres of County Down, a compact granite range that feels separate from the rest of Ireland. Unlike Wicklow's high plateaus or Atlantic cliffs, the Mournes are steep-sided summits with deep valleys. Ancient, hard granite has resisted erosion through millennia. You step out of a seaside resort, walk an hour into true mountain wilderness, and you're alone in granite and sky. Slieve Donard is the emotional focal point. The standard approach from Newcastle follows the Glen River path through woodland, then breaks onto open mountainside with 750m ascent. A good day takes 4–5 hours return. The summit holds two ancient burial cairns, including the oldest and highest passage tomb in Ireland. On clear days the view ranges from the Scottish coast to the Hills of Wales. The Mourne Wall: Monument to Labour. Built 1904–1922 by the Belfast Water Commissioners, the Mourne Wall stretches 35km across 15 peaks, enclosing the 9,000-acre Silent Valley catchment. Granite dry-stone built by hundreds of stonemasons working each year, the project took 18 years and became one of the great mountain engineering achievements in these islands.
short_description: Northern Ireland's finest mountain range — granite peaks, the remarkable Mourne Wall linking 15 summits, and Slieve Donard rising 852m above the Irish Sea with views to five countries on a clear day.
overview: County Down's secret: the Mourne Mountains. Just an hour south of Belfast, this compact granite range of 15 major peaks rises dramatically from the coast, entirely different from rolling Wicklow hills. Slieve Donard at 852m is Northern Ireland's highest peak. On clear days the summit offers views to five countries: Ireland, Scotland, England, the Isle of Man and Wales. The Mourne Wall, a 35km dry-stone structure built 1904–1922, links 15 peaks. Hundreds of stonemasons carved granite blocks to enclose Silent Valley and protect Belfast's water supply. Newcastle is the obvious base: a Victorian seaside resort with good accommodation. Beyond the high ridges lies Tollymore Forest Park's ancient woodland, Silent Valley Reservoir, Rostrevor where C.S. Lewis found Narnia inspiration, and Murlough's sand dunes. This is mountain walking where landscape connects to history, literature and engineering.
landscape_description: The Mourne Mountains are ancient, hard granite that resists weathering. Summits are topped with granite tors, rocky outcrops weathered into dramatic shapes. Steep-sided valleys and sharp ridgelines differ from glacially carved Wicklow valleys. In Tollymore's woodland valleys, oak and ash dominate, with Victorian-imported redwoods and exotic firs. Higher up, heather and cross-leaved heath colour the moorland purple and orange in late summer. Alpine plants cling to exposed ground near the summits. Sika deer inhabit woodlands; peregrine falcons hunt cliff faces; seals haul out on Murlough beaches. The coast contrast is striking. Newcastle beach is flat and wide with golden sand stretching kilometres. Murlough offers rolling sand dunes. Walking from forest to mountain to coast in a single day crosses entirely different ecological zones.
cultural_highlights: County Down has been shaped by 1,500 years of Irish history. Saint Patrick is central; tradition holds he died and is buried at Downpatrick Cathedral. The Mourne Wall, built 1904 to 1922, is a monument to labour: hundreds of stonemasons worked exposed ridges, cutting and carrying granite blocks. Walking alongside, you touch stonework shaped by human hands across two decades. C.S. Lewis spent childhood holidays at Rostrevor; the Mourne Mountains appear throughout his writing. The view overlooking Carlingford Lough was his "idea of Narnia." Percy French's song, The Mountains of Mourne (1896), is the most famous cultural reference. Mourne seafood from Kilkeel — fresh fish in every coastal restaurant. Mourne Seafood Bar, Newcastle — post-walk dinner spot with daily catches. Ulster fry — proper full Irish breakfast before a big day on Slieve Donard. Local lamb — pasture-raised on the mountain slopes in hearty stews
difficulty_overview: The Mourne Mountains offer walking at multiple difficulty levels, from gentle forest walks to serious mountain terrain. Easy to Moderate: Tollymore Forest Park circuits (8–12km, minimal ascent), Newcastle to Murlough coastal walk (8–10km, flat), and Silent Valley circuit (5–8km, low elevation gain). These suit families and walkers with modest fitness. Challenging: Slieve Donard summit (4–5 hours, 750m ascent) and sections of the Mourne Wall over multiple peaks. These require good fitness, sure-footedness on rocky terrain and full mountain navigation experience. Ridges are exposed; weather changes rapidly. Expert-only: The full Mourne Wall circuit (35km over 15 summits) is a serious mountain undertaking requiring full mountain experience and excellent weather.
accommodation_style: Newcastle is the accommodation hub: a Victorian seaside resort with options from budget hostels to luxury hotels. The Slieve Donard Hotel (1898) is the flagship: a red-brick Victorian building with sea views, luxury spa and fine dining. The Burrendale Hotel and Spa offers four-star comfort at a lower price point. Smaller guesthouses and B&Bs are family-run and welcoming to walkers. Rostrevor on Carlingford Lough offers a quieter alternative with excellent accommodation. We pre-book quality accommodation with breakfast included. Luggage is transferred each morning.
practical_info: Belfast International Airport (BFS) is the primary arrival point; a 60 kilometre drive south via the M1 motorway reaches Newcastle in approximately 60 to 75 minutes on the scenic A24 descent toward the coast. From Belfast city centre, Translink NI operates regular bus services to Newcastle Bus Station (75 to 90 minutes). Rental cars are readily available at both Belfast airports. Northern Ireland Railways offers limited rail services; the nearest station is Bangor, but services to County Down are minimal. Most walkers arrive by car or Translink bus. Northern Ireland uses British Sterling (GBP).
who_is_it_for: The Mourne Mountains suit experienced hillwalkers wanting genuine mountain terrain in Northern Ireland. High peaks require good fitness, mountain navigation skills and proper equipment. They work well for walkers wanting to base themselves in one location and take day walks of varying difficulty: challenging ridge walking on Slieve Donard one day, gentle forest walks in Tollymore or coast walks at Murlough the next. Couples, friends and solo walkers thrive with this flexibility. Families can enjoy forest and coastal sections while keeping high mountains for experienced parents. The Mournes suit walkers wanting mountain depth combined with historical richness: the Mourne Wall engineering, the C.S. Lewis connection, and St Patrick's pilgrimage.
best_time_to_visit: May, June and September are optimal. May brings long evenings, wildflowers on moorland and lighter traffic. June and early July offer the longest daylight. September is arguably finest; the air is clearer, light is low and golden, heather blooms across the moorland, and accommodation is easier to book. July and August are busier: coastal towns fill with visitors and accommodation needs advance booking. Weather is generally stable but afternoon cloud is common. Winter is possible on lower routes but high ridges require experienced walkers in stable, dry conditions.

name: The Sperrin Mountains & Mid Ulster
description: The Landscape and the Gold. The Sperrin Mountains form Northern Ireland's largest upland area, shaped by ancient metamorphic rock; schist and quartzite create ridges stretching 80km. Sawel Mountain at 678m is the highest point, offering unending views across Ulster. Gold is woven into the Sperrin story. Traces have been found in streams for thousands of years. Bronze Age metalworkers knew it was here. Geological surveys in the 1970s confirmed substantial deposits, still recovered from Sperrin streams. The broad moorland ridges differ from other Irish ranges. Unlike Wicklow's granite peaks or Antrim's basalt cliffs, the Sperrins unfold in long horizontal lines. Blanket bog covers summits; deep, waterlogged peat requiring good navigation in mist. Valleys like Glenelly and Owenkillew offer sheltered walking through farmland, forestry and river corridors. Wildlife is often seen at dawn or dusk. Red grouse flush from heather. Hen harriers hunt the moorland. Red kites recently returned to Ulster. Valleys support otters, badgers in oak woodland and foxes. Spring woods fill with redstarts, wood warblers and treecreepers.
short_description: Broad moorland ridges, ancient gold-bearing rock and the most open skies in Northern Ireland — the Sperrins are an upland wilderness where solitude feels like a luxury.
overview: The Sperrin Mountains stretch across 80 kilometres of Mid Ulster, from County Derry/Londonderry in the north to County Tyrone in the south. They are Northern Ireland's largest upland area and genuinely underrated. While the Mournes draw crowds and the Antrim coast fills with tourists, the Sperrins offer something we love: genuine solitude and wilderness. The moorland horizons stretch for miles, and peace comes from walking where almost no one else ventures. The mountains have distinctive horizontal character; ridgelines are long and rolling rather than craggy, summits are blanket bog rather than bare rock. It's a landscape that rewards slowness and reflection, where open skies reward clear days and mist creates intimate walking.
landscape_description: The Sperrin Mountains span over 1,000 square kilometres across Mid Ulster as Northern Ireland's largest upland area. Unlike the Mourne Mountains with dramatic granite cones or the Antrim plateau with basalt cliffs, the Sperrins have distinctive horizontal character with broad, rolling ridgelines and uninterrupted moorland stretching kilometres. Metamorphic rock resists the sharp peaks that characterise other Irish ranges. Mountains rise in long moorland ridges separated by deep river valleys like Glenelly and Owenkillew, offering sheltered walking through farmland, oak woodland and river flats. Upper sections are mantled in blanket bog where views are unobstructed. The light has particular quality; open moorland lets the eye travel further than in other ranges. On clear days, you see across all Northern Ireland. Mist comes fast and the landscape becomes intimate. Seasonal changes are pronounced: spring brings breeding birds, summer turns heather purple, autumn colours the bracken. Sika deer, red grouse, golden plover, hen harriers and reintroduced red kites populate the landscape. Valleys support otters, badgers and foxes.
cultural_highlights: The Sperrins are layered with history. Bronze Age monuments, stone circles, cairns and standing stones suggest an ancient people who found this landscape sacred. Beaghmore Stone Circles, seven Bronze Age circles, suggest ritual or astronomical significance; walking among them means walking through genuine prehistory. The linen industry marks the medieval period. The 18th and 19th centuries saw massive emigration to America; the Ulster American Folk Park tells this story through costumed guides and authentic buildings. Wellbrook Beetling Mill connects the landscape to Ulster's industrial heritage, with machinery in seasonal operation bringing that past into the present. Sperrin lamb and mountain mutton — pasture-raised on moorland, exceptional quality in local pubs. Ulster fry — the full cooked breakfast with sausage, bacon, egg and soda bread. Tyrone farmhouse cheeses — small-scale producers creating cheeses of distinction. Potato dishes — champ, boxty and colcannon; staple Mid Ulster fare
difficulty_overview: Walking in the Sperrins is moderate overall, varying by route. Valley trails are gentle and suitable for families. The main Sperrin Way and higher mountain routes require reasonable fitness and proper hill walking skills. The greatest challenge is navigation. Open moorland can be disorienting in mist or poor light. Blanket bog underfoot is waterlogged even in dry periods. Waterproof footwear with good ankle support is essential. A detailed 1:25,000 OS map or GPS tracks are highly recommended for mountain sections. Sperrin Way waymarking is generally good, though open moorland sections require careful attention.
accommodation_style: Accommodation spreads across small market towns: Gortin, Draperstown, Dungiven, Plumbridge, Cookstown and Omagh, rather than concentrating in one location. The accommodation is family-run bed and breakfasts and small guesthouses. This dispersed model is part of the Sperrins' appeal; you stay in real villages and experience contemporary rural Ulster life. We pre-book quality accommodation with breakfast at each stop. Luggage transfer is arranged daily; you walk carrying only what you need.
practical_info: Belfast International Airport (BFS) is the primary arrival point with direct services from Europe and North America. Airlink express bus connects the airport to the city centre in approximately 30 minutes. From Belfast, Omagh is approximately 90 minutes by car or bus. City of Derry Airport (LDY) serves the western Sperrins, approximately 45 minutes from western Sperrin trailheads. Translink NI operates express services from Belfast Buscentre to Omagh (approximately 2 hours) or from Dublin (approximately 3 hours). By train: Train services run from Belfast Central and Dublin Connolly to Derry; approximately 2 hours from Belfast, 3.5 hours from Dublin. By car: Omagh is on the A5 between Belfast and Dublin; Derry is on the N13 route. All Sperrin trailheads are within 30 to 45 minutes' drive of these towns.
who_is_it_for: The Sperrins suit walkers seeking genuine solitude and wilderness without crowds. If trails like the Wicklow Way feel too busy or you want a genuinely remote mountain landscape, the Sperrins deliver. The range suits experienced hill walkers comfortable with open moorland navigation and variable weather, and equally suits valley walkers seeking easier terrain. Solo walkers, couples and small groups thrive here. The lack of crowds appeals to anyone seeking genuine peace. The Sperrins are less ideal for walkers entirely new to hill walking; proper navigation skills are important.
best_time_to_visit: May, June and September are optimal. May brings long daylight and wildflowers on moorland. June offers the longest evenings. September is arguably finest; the light is golden and clear, heather blooms across ridges, accommodation is easier to book and trails are quieter than July and August. August sees heather in full bloom; the moorland turns purple. July and August are busier, though "busy" means far fewer walkers than on famous trails. The mountains are walkable year-round but winter above 400 metres requires mountain experience and cold-weather gear.
